questions.question: In hexagonal architecture, which direction must dependencies point?
options: From adapters toward the domain core
From the domain core toward adapters
In both directions — core depends on adapters and vice versa
Dependencies should be minimized but direction doesn't matter
questions.explanation: The dependency rule in hexagonal architecture is absolute: dependencies point inward. The domain core defines port interfaces; adapters depend on those interfaces. The core never imports anything from an adapter. This inversion is what makes the core testable and technology-independent.

questions.question: In a traditional layered architecture (Presentation → Business → Data), what causes the biggest problem?
options: The presentation layer is too thin
The business layer depends on the data-access layer, coupling domain logic to infrastructure
Layers cannot communicate with each other
There are too many layers
questions.explanation: In layered architecture, the Business Logic layer directly imports repository concretions from the Data Access layer. This means business rules depend on SQL, ORM entities, and connection management. Testing a business rule requires a database. Changing storage requires changing business code. Hexagonal architecture fixes this by inverting the dependency through port interfaces.

questions.question: What is the difference between a driving port and a driven port in hexagonal architecture?
options: A driving port is implemented by the core; a driven port is implemented by external services
A driving port defines what the application CAN do (its API); a driven port defines what the application NEEDS (its SPI)
A driving port handles HTTP requests; a driven port handles database queries
Driving ports are synchronous; driven ports are asynchronous
questions.explanation: A driving (primary) port is the interface through which outside callers invoke the application — e.g., RegisterUserUseCase. A driven (secondary) port is the interface through which the application invokes external systems — e.g., UserRepository. The key distinction is direction of the call: outside→core (driving) vs. core→outside (driven). Both are defined by the core.

questions.question: Why can you test a hexagonal core using an InMemoryUserRepository instead of a real database?
options: Because the InMemoryUserRepository is a mock that bypasses business logic
Because the core depends on the UserRepository port interface, not on PostgreSQL — the adapter is swappable
Because in-memory repositories are always faster and more correct than real databases
Because hexagonal architecture eliminates the need for integration tests
questions.explanation: The core defines the UserRepository interface. InMemoryUserRepository implements that interface. Because the use case only knows about the interface, you can substitute any implementation — in-memory for fast unit tests, PostgreSQL for integration tests. This isn't mocking that bypasses logic; it's substituting the infrastructure while the same business logic runs unchanged.

questions.question: How does Clean Architecture's dependency rule relate to hexagonal architecture's port/adapter pattern?
options: They are contradictory — Clean Architecture forbids interfaces at the boundary
They express the same dependency rule; Clean Architecture adds a four-layer naming convention (entities, use cases, interface adapters, frameworks) on top of the same inward-pointing principle
Clean Architecture only applies to Java; hexagonal architecture only applies to functional languages
They are completely unrelated patterns for different problems
questions.explanation: Both architectures enforce the same fundamental rule: dependencies point inward. Hexagonal architecture emphasizes ports and adapters as the mechanism. Clean Architecture adds a four-layer structure (Entities, Use Cases, Interface Adapters, Frameworks & Drivers) that maps to the hexagon: Entities = core, Use Cases = application services, Interface Adapters = adapter implementations, Frameworks = infrastructure. The naming differs, the principle is identical.

questions.question: A team builds a user service where the User entity has JPA @Entity annotations and the use case directly calls EntityManager.persist(). Which architectural rule is violated?
options: Open/Closed Principle — the entity is open for extension
Dependency Rule — the domain core depends on a framework (JPA), so dependencies point outward
Single Responsibility — the entity has too many fields
Interface Segregation — the entity implements too many interfaces
questions.explanation: JPA's @Entity annotation and EntityManager are infrastructure concerns (frameworks & drivers layer). When the domain core imports javax.persistence, it violates the dependency rule: the core now depends on an outer circle. The fix: define a UserRepository port interface in the core, implement it with a JpaUserRepository adapter in the outer layer. The core stays framework-free.
